note_data.title: Alcohols, esters and carboxylic acids
keyPoints: **Primary alcohols** are oxidised by acidified potassium dichromate(VI) to aldehydes then carboxylic acids
**Secondary alcohols** are oxidised by acidified potassium dichromate(VI) to ketones only
**Tertiary alcohols** are not oxidised by acidified potassium dichromate(VI) under normal conditions
**Dehydration of alcohols** occurs by heating with concentrated sulfuric acid or passing over heated aluminium oxide catalyst to form alkenes
**Fermentation** produces ethanol from glucose using yeast enzymes in anaerobic conditions at 30-40°C
**Esters** are formed by condensation reaction between carboxylic acids and alcohols in presence of concentrated sulfuric acid catalyst
**Hydrolysis of esters** occurs by heating with dilute hydrochloric acid (acid hydrolysis) or sodium hydroxide (alkaline hydrolysis/saponification)
**Carboxylic acids** show typical acid properties: react with metals, carbonates, and alkalis to form salts
**Reduction of carboxylic acids** occurs using lithium aluminium hydride in dry ether to form primary alcohols

definitions.definition: The breakdown of glucose by enzymes in yeast in the absence of oxygen to produce ethanol and carbon dioxide
A condensation reaction between a carboxylic acid and an alcohol in the presence of an acid catalyst to form an ester and water
The alkaline hydrolysis of an ester using sodium hydroxide to form a carboxylate salt and an alcohol
The removal of water from an alcohol molecule to form an alkene

howToAnswer.instruction: Give the reagent and conditions only. No mechanism or explanation needed.
Give the observation and link to the chemical change using 'because' or 'due to'
Give the key steps in logical order with reagents and conditions

modelAnswer.question: Describe how you would distinguish between a primary, secondary and tertiary alcohol using chemical tests. Include reagents, conditions and observations.
modelAnswer.answer: Add acidified potassium dichromate(VI) and warm gently. Primary alcohol: orange solution turns green due to formation of aldehyde then carboxylic acid. Secondary alcohol: orange solution turns green due to formation of ketone. Tertiary alcohol: orange solution remains orange as no oxidation occurs under normal conditions.
markBreakdown: Mark 3: Primary alcohol observation - orange to green
Mark 4: Secondary alcohol observation - orange to green
Mark 5: Tertiary alcohol observation - remains orange
Mark 6: Explanation of different oxidation products

commonMistakes: Never say 'potassium dichromate' - always say 'acidified potassium dichromate(VI)' for oxidation reactions
Students often confuse aldehyde and ketone formation - primary alcohols give aldehydes, secondary give ketones
Don't say esters are formed by 'addition' - they are formed by condensation with elimination of water
Never write 'reflux' for fermentation conditions - fermentation occurs at 30-40°C in anaerobic conditions
Students forget that tertiary alcohols cannot be oxidised under normal laboratory conditions
examinerTips: Alcohol oxidation appears in almost every Paper 2 - learn reagents and conditions precisely
Always include 'acidified' when writing potassium dichromate(VI) as oxidising agent
Esterification questions often ask for reversible arrow and catalyst in equation
Learn the difference between acid and alkaline hydrolysis of esters - different products formed
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Oxidation flowchart showing primary alcohol → aldehyde → carboxylic acid, and secondary alcohol → ketone (no further oxidation). Include reagents: acidified K₂Cr₂O₇, conditions: heat/warm, and colour change: orange to green.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to write equations for the formation of ethyl ethanoate from ethanoic acid and ethanol, name the type of reaction, and explain why concentrated sulfuric acid is used
O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question asking which compound is produced when propanol is oxidized by acidified potassium manganate(VII)
M/J 2023 P2 Q6c (4 marks) — students had to explain why carboxylic acids are weak acids compared to hydrochloric acid, using equations to support their answer
O/N 2022 P2 Q3b (3 marks) — asked for the structural formula of the ester formed between methanoic acid and butan-1-ol, including naming the ester
M/J 2024 P1 Q18 (1 mark) — identification of the functional group present in compounds like CH₃CH₂COOH

workedExample.question: Ethanol can be converted to ethanoic acid. (a) Name the type of reaction. (1 mark) (b) Write a balanced symbol equation for this reaction using [O] to represent the oxidizing agent. (2 marks) (c) Name a suitable oxidizing agent for this reaction. (1 mark)
steps: Step 1: Identify the reaction type - alcohol to carboxylic acid involves adding oxygen, so this is oxidation
Step 2: Write the equation starting with ethanol (C₂H₅OH) and showing it converts to ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH)
Step 3: Balance the equation: C₂H₅OH + 2[O] → CH₃COOH + H₂O
Step 4: Name a suitable oxidizing agent - acidified potassium manganate(VII) or acidified potassium dichromate(VI)
workedExample.answer: (a) Oxidation (b) C₂H₅OH + 2[O] → CH₃COOH + H₂O (c) Acidified potassium manganate(VII) OR acidified potassium dichromate(VI)
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for 'oxidation', 1 mark for correct reactants and products, 1 mark for balancing, 1 mark for correct oxidizing agent with 'acidified'

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates wrote the molecular formula instead of structural formula for esters' — always draw the full structural formula showing the -COO- link when asked for ester structures

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates frequently forgot to mention that sulfuric acid acts as a catalyst in esterification' — always state that concentrated H₂SO₄ is a catalyst and explain it speeds up the reaction when discussing ester formation
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students often wrote that carboxylic acids completely ionize in water' — remember that carboxylic acids are WEAK acids that only partially ionize, unlike strong acids like HCl
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw the esterification reaction mechanism showing: (1) Ethanoic acid (CH₃COOH) + Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) with structural formulas, (2) Arrow pointing right labeled 'conc. H₂SO₄ catalyst, heat', (3) Products: ethyl ethanoate (CH₃COOC₂H₅) + water (H₂O) with full structural formulas, (4) Double-headed arrow to show the reaction is reversible. Include all bonds, especially highlighting the C-O-O-C ester link formation.
note_data.quickRecap: Alcohols oxidize to carboxylic acids using acidified KMnO₄. Carboxylic acids are weak acids that partially ionize in water. Esters form when carboxylic acids react with alcohols using concentrated H₂SO₄ catalyst - this is a reversible condensation reaction producing water. Always draw full structural formulas showing the -COO- ester linkage.
